1263:       subroutine give3( axis, dir, u, n1, n2, n3, k )


[...]


1282:       buff_id = 2 + dir 


1283:       buff_len = 0


1284: 


1285:       if( axis .eq.  1 )then


1286:          if( dir .eq. -1 )then


1287: 


1288:             do  i3=2,n3-1


1289:                do  i2=2,n2-1


1290:                   buff_len = buff_len + 1


1291:                   buff(buff_len,buff_id ) = u( 2,  i2,i3)


1292:                enddo


1293:             enddo


1294: 


1295:             if (timeron) call timer_start(t_comm3)


1296:             call mpi_send(  &


1297:      &           buff(1, buff_id ), buff_len,dp_type,  &


1298:      &           nbr( axis, dir, k ), msg_type(axis,dir),  &


1299:      &           comm_work, ierr)


1300:             if (timeron) call timer_stop(t_comm3)


1301: 


1302:          else if( dir .eq. +1 ) then


1303:

1305:                do  i2=2,n2-1


1306:                   buff_len = buff_len + 1


1307:                   buff(buff_len, buff_id ) = u( n1-1, i2,i3)


1308:                enddo


1309:             enddo

Vectorization


Your function is not vectorized.
Only 18% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 120.00 to 16.20 cycles (7.41x speedup).

Details


All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:

    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

  • timer_start: 6 occurrences►

Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

No instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on FP elements. This function is probably writing/copying data or processing integer elements.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 120.00 cycles. At this rate:

  • 6% of peak load performance is reached (12.87 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 4% of peak store performance is reached (5.63 out of 128.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 120.00 to 83.67 cycles (1.43x speedup).

Vectorization


Your function is not vectorized.
Only 18% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 120.00 to 16.20 cycles (7.41x speedup).

Details


All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:

    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

No instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on FP elements. This function is probably writing/copying data or processing integer elements.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 120.00 cycles. At this rate:

  • 6% of peak load performance is reached (12.87 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 4% of peak store performance is reached (5.63 out of 128.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 120.00 to 83.67 cycles (1.43x speedup).
